policy_statement: Assess co-benefits of ecosystems in marine protected areas for mitigation and adaptation and implement actions to strengthen these co-benefits (I12).
mechanism_description: The policy will assess mitigation and adaptation co-benefits of ecosystems in marine protected areas and implement actions that strengthen ecosystem services such as carbon storage and climate resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem conservation/enhancement → ↑ carbon sinks + avoided carbon release (blue carbon) → ↓ net GHG emissions/↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Designate/manage marine protected areas and implement conservation/restoration measures that reduce damaging activities (e.g., dredging, destructive fishing, coastal development impacts) and improve habitat condition.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Healthier coastal/marine vegetated ecosystems accumulate and store carbon in biomass and especially sediments; protection also prevents disturbance/oxidation of stored sediment carbon and supports ecosystem integrity.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased long-term carbon sequestration and storage (“blue carbon”) and reduced emissions from ecosystem loss/disturbance; secondarily, improved climate resilience via coastal protection, biodiversity, and fisheries stability.
dominant_pathway_test: Protecting and enhancing marine ecosystems most strongly affects climate by preserving/increasing blue‑carbon sequestration and avoiding GHG emissions from habitat degradation/disturbance.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection/restoration → maintains/enhances carbon sequestration and prevents release of stored carbon → lowers net GHGs.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the policy explicitly mentions mitigation and adaptation co-benefits, the mechanism’s core lever is ecosystem conservation/enhancement (a nature-based intervention), with carbon preservation as the main climate-relevant effect across instances.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an MPA is created mainly for disaster risk reduction (wave attenuation) with negligible carbon implications, it could lean Adaptation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets carbon preservation/storage, keeping Nature-Based Solutions dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy framed as marine ecosystem conservation/enhancement for ecosystem and carbon preservation relies on biological carbon sinks/avoided release as the primary climate lever.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a specific policy is more “protection” vs “restoration,” and the balance of mitigation vs adaptation benefits, requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some marine protection actions (e.g., fisheries-only measures or general biodiversity MPAs) may deliver minimal measurable carbon benefits; classification assumes the mechanism includes explicit blue-carbon preservation/enhancement (seagrass/mangroves/saltmarsh/kelp, sediment protection) as stated.
key_indicators: “will be assessed”; “with respect to climate change mitigation and adaptation”; “Actions will also be implemented to strengthen these co-benefits”; “marine protected areas”; “co-benefits of different ecosystems”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; fisheries productivity; coastal water quality; shoreline/coastal protection and disaster risk reduction
instance_edge_case_notes: “Actions will also be implemented” is non-specific; depending on the eventual actions, this could later map to regulatory-mandate (activity restrictions), infrastructure-investment (restoration), or performance-standard/MRV, but as written it is best classified as a planning/assessment-led strategy.
